name: Environmental Science
description: An interdisciplinary study of Earth's ecosystems, human impact on the environment, natural resources, climate science, and sustainable solutions — preparing students to think critically about our planet's future.

modules.title: Introduction to Environmental Science
modules.objectives: Apply the scientific method to environmental questions; explain systems thinking and trace key events in environmental history.
modules.readingNote: National Geographic Education — Environmental Science Resource Library
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Environmental Science — What Is Environmental Science?
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Ecology #1 — Introduction to Ecology
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Environmental Science practice
modules.assignment: Identify a local environmental issue. Describe how you would design a scientific study to investigate it, including hypothesis, variables, and data collection method.

modules.title: Ecosystems & Biomes
modules.objectives: Distinguish biotic from abiotic factors; construct a food web; explain energy flow and identify major terrestrial biomes.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Environmental Science — Food Chains & Food Webs
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Food Chains & Food Webs practice
modules.assignment: Create a detailed food web for one major biome. Identify all trophic levels and explain what would happen if the top predator were removed.

modules.title: Population Ecology
modules.objectives: Explain exponential and logistic growth models; define carrying capacity; analyze human population trends.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Environmental Science — Population Growth & Regulation
modules.assignment: Graph both exponential and logistic growth curves for a hypothetical population. Label the carrying capacity and explain what limits further growth.

modules.title: Biodiversity & Conservation
modules.objectives: Define species diversity and habitat loss; identify threats to biodiversity; compare conservation strategies.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Environmental Science — Biodiversity & Natural Capital
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — Why Is Biodiversity So Important?
modules.assignment: Research one endangered species. Describe the threats it faces, what conservation strategies are in place, and evaluate their effectiveness.

modules.title: Soil & Land Resources
modules.objectives: Describe soil formation and horizon layers; explain causes of erosion; evaluate sustainable agriculture practices.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Environmental Science — Soil Formation
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — Can We Create the Perfect Farm?
modules.assignment: Draw and label the major soil horizons (O, A, B, C, R). Explain how each layer forms and identify two human activities that degrade soil quality.

modules.title: Water Resources
modules.objectives: Trace the water cycle; distinguish surface water from groundwater; analyze causes and effects of water pollution and scarcity.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Environmental Science — Water Supply & Use
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How Much of the Earth Is Actually Habitable?
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Water Use & Management practice
modules.assignment: Diagram the full water cycle with labels. Identify three human activities that disrupt it and propose one conservation strategy for each.

modules.title: Atmosphere & Air Quality
modules.objectives: Name the layers of the atmosphere and their properties; identify major air pollutants; explain how smog and acid rain form.
modules.readingNote: NOAA SciJinks — Layers of the Atmosphere
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Environmental Science — Air Pollution
modules.assignment: Create a diagram of the atmospheric layers. For each major air pollutant (SO₂, NOₓ, PM2.5, O₃), identify its source and one health or environmental effect.

modules.title: Climate Science
modules.objectives: Explain the greenhouse effect and how greenhouse gases trap heat; distinguish weather from climate; describe evidence of climate change.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Environmental Science — The Greenhouse Effect
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — What Is the Greenhouse Effect?
modules.assignment: List five lines of scientific evidence for climate change. For each, explain what the data shows and why it is significant.

modules.title: Climate Change Impacts & Policy
modules.objectives: Describe major impacts of climate change including sea level rise and extreme weather; evaluate international climate agreements including the Paris Accord.
modules.readingNote: NOAA — Sea Level Rise Technical Report
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Environmental Science — Consequences of Global Warming
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — Can We Save the Ocean One Degree at a Time?
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Consequences of Climate Change practice
modules.assignment: Analyze the Paris Agreement: What are its goals? Which countries are the largest emitters? Evaluate whether current pledges are sufficient to limit warming to 1.5°C.

modules.title: Fossil Fuels & Energy
modules.objectives: Explain how fossil fuels form and are extracted; calculate the environmental costs of combustion; compare coal, oil, and natural gas.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Environmental Science — Fossil Fuels
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How Do We Turn Fossil Fuels into Power?
modules.assignment: Compare coal, oil, and natural gas across five dimensions: energy density, CO₂ emissions per unit, primary uses, reserves, and major environmental risks.

modules.title: Renewable Energy
modules.objectives: Describe how solar, wind, hydroelectric, geothermal, and nuclear energy work; evaluate the pros and cons of each source.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Environmental Science — Renewable Energy Sources
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — The 5 Big Questions in Renewable Energy
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Renewable Energy Sources practice
modules.assignment: Create a comparison table for five renewable energy sources (solar, wind, hydro, geothermal, nuclear) evaluating cost, reliability, land use, environmental impact, and scalability.

modules.title: Pollution: Types & Sources
modules.objectives: Classify types of pollution (water, air, soil, plastic/ocean); distinguish point from non-point sources; identify major pollutants and their pathways.
modules.readingNote: NOAA — Marine Debris: Ocean Plastic Pollution
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Environmental Science — Water Pollution
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How Plastic Pollution Is Affecting Our Oceans
modules.assignment: Choose one major pollution event (e.g., Deepwater Horizon, Minamata). Describe the source, type of pollution, environmental impact, and how it was or could have been prevented.

modules.title: Waste Management & Circular Economy
modules.objectives: Apply the reduce/reuse/recycle hierarchy; describe landfill design and hazardous waste management; explain life-cycle analysis and circular economy principles.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Environmental Science — Solid Waste
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — What Really Happens to the Plastic You Throw Away?
modules.assignment: Conduct a personal waste audit for one day. Categorize your waste into recyclable, compostable, hazardous, and landfill. Propose three specific changes to reduce your waste.

modules.title: Sustainability & Environmental Solutions
modules.titleZh: 可持续发展与环境解决方案
modules.objectives: Define sustainable development; explain environmental justice; evaluate green technologies; identify meaningful individual and collective actions.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Environmental Science — Solving Global Environmental Problems
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — Is It Too Late to Stop Climate Change?
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Solving Global Change practice
modules.assignment: Design a sustainability plan for your school. Address energy, water, waste, and transportation. Use data to justify each proposed change and estimate its environmental impact.

questions.question: Which of the following best defines an ecosystem?
options: A group of organisms of the same species
A community of living organisms and their physical environment interacting as a system
The physical environment without living organisms
A single food chain in a habitat
questions.answer: A community of living organisms and their physical environment interacting as a system

questions.question: In a food web, which organisms occupy the first trophic level?
questions.answer: Producers (plants, algae)

questions.question: Carrying capacity is defined as:
options: The total mass of organisms in an ecosystem
The maximum population size an environment can sustain indefinitely
The birth rate minus death rate
The minimum number needed to avoid extinction
questions.answer: The maximum population size an environment can sustain indefinitely

questions.question: Which factor is abiotic?
questions.answer: Temperature of a pond

questions.question: The primary cause of habitat loss worldwide is:
options: Human land conversion for agriculture and development
questions.answer: Human land conversion for agriculture and development

questions.question: Which soil horizon contains the most organic matter?
questions.answer: A horizon (topsoil)

questions.question: The water cycle process by which water vapor enters the atmosphere from plant leaves is called:
questions.answer: Transpiration

questions.question: Acid rain forms primarily when which gases react with water in the atmosphere?
questions.answer: SO₂ and NOₓ

questions.question: Which layer of the atmosphere contains the ozone layer?
questions.answer: Stratosphere

questions.question: Logistic growth of a population is characterized by:
options: An S-shaped curve leveling off at carrying capacity
A J-shaped curve with no limit
questions.answer: An S-shaped curve leveling off at carrying capacity

questions.question: A keystone species is one that:
options: Has the largest population in an ecosystem
Has a disproportionately large effect on its ecosystem relative to its abundance
questions.answer: Has a disproportionately large effect on its ecosystem relative to its abundance

questions.question: Which practice best prevents soil erosion?
questions.answer: Contour plowing and cover crops

questions.question: Explain the difference between a point source and a non-point source of water pollution. Give one example of each and explain why non-point sources are harder to regulate.
questions.answer: A point source is a single, identifiable source of pollution such as a factory discharge pipe or sewage outfall. A non-point source is diffuse pollution from many locations, such as agricultural runoff carrying fertilizers and pesticides from fields across a watershed. Non-point sources are harder to regulate because there is no single origin to monitor or control — the pollution enters water bodies from countless locations over wide areas, making enforcement and treatment difficult.

questions.question: Eutrophication of water bodies is primarily caused by excess:
questions.answer: Nitrogen and phosphorus from fertilizers

questions.question: Describe how systems thinking applies to environmental science. Use one example of a feedback loop (positive or negative) found in nature to illustrate your answer.
questions.answer: Systems thinking is an approach that examines how components of a complex system interact and influence one another, rather than studying parts in isolation. In environmental science, ecosystems are viewed as interconnected webs of relationships. A negative feedback loop example: as a deer population grows, food (vegetation) becomes scarce; starvation causes the population to decline; vegetation recovers; and the cycle stabilizes. This self-regulating mechanism is a negative feedback loop that helps maintain ecosystem balance.

questions.question: The greenhouse effect occurs when:
options: Sunlight is blocked by greenhouse structures
Atmospheric gases absorb and re-emit infrared radiation, trapping heat
The ozone layer reflects heat back to Earth
questions.answer: Atmospheric gases absorb and re-emit infrared radiation, trapping heat

questions.question: Which greenhouse gas has the highest global warming potential per molecule?
questions.answer: Nitrous oxide (N₂O)

questions.question: Sea level rise is caused primarily by:
options: Thermal expansion of seawater and melting land ice
questions.answer: Thermal expansion of seawater and melting land ice

questions.question: The Paris Agreement aims to limit global temperature rise to no more than:
questions.answer: 1.5–2°C above pre-industrial levels

questions.question: Fossil fuels are considered non-renewable because:
options: They take millions of years to form and are consumed faster than they are replenished
They are found only in a few countries
They are too expensive to extract
questions.answer: They take millions of years to form and are consumed faster than they are replenished

questions.question: Which renewable energy source produces electricity using the heat of Earth's interior?
questions.answer: Geothermal energy

questions.question: The 'reduce, reuse, recycle' hierarchy prioritizes reduction first because:
options: Reducing waste prevents the need for more energy-intensive reuse or recycling
Recycling is impossible for most materials
questions.answer: Reducing waste prevents the need for more energy-intensive reuse or recycling

questions.question: Environmental justice refers to:
options: The fair distribution of environmental benefits and burdens across all communities regardless of race or income
questions.answer: The fair distribution of environmental benefits and burdens across all communities regardless of race or income

questions.question: Which of the following is a non-renewable energy source?
questions.answer: Natural gas

questions.question: The largest source of freshwater on Earth is:
questions.answer: Glaciers and ice caps

questions.question: Biomagnification is the process by which:
options: Plants grow larger near pollution sources
Toxins become more concentrated at higher trophic levels in a food chain
Pollutants break down faster in large organisms
questions.answer: Toxins become more concentrated at higher trophic levels in a food chain

questions.question: Sustainable development is defined as development that:
options: Meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs
Prioritizes economic growth over environmental concerns
questions.answer: Meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs

questions.question: Which of these best describes the circular economy model?
options: A model in which resources are extracted, used, and discarded
A system that keeps resources in use as long as possible, recovers materials, and regenerates natural systems
An economy based only on recycled products
A trade model based on circular trade routes
questions.answer: A system that keeps resources in use as long as possible, recovers materials, and regenerates natural systems

questions.question: Ozone depletion in the stratosphere is primarily caused by:
questions.answer: Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs)

questions.question: Which energy source has the lowest lifecycle carbon emissions per kilowatt-hour?
questions.answer: Nuclear

questions.question: Explain how deforestation contributes to both climate change and biodiversity loss. What is one policy or practice that could address both issues simultaneously?
questions.answer: Deforestation contributes to climate change because trees store carbon; when they are cut and burned, CO₂ is released into the atmosphere, accelerating global warming. It also contributes to biodiversity loss because forests are habitats for more than half of all terrestrial species — clearing them destroys habitat, fragments ecosystems, and drives species to extinction. REDD+ (Reducing Emissions from Deforestation and Forest Degradation) is a UN program that pays developing countries to protect their forests, addressing both issues simultaneously by keeping carbon stored in trees while preserving habitat.

questions.question: An ecological footprint measures:
options: The area of land a species occupies
The total amount of biologically productive land and water needed to support a human lifestyle
The depth of soil in agricultural regions
questions.answer: The total amount of biologically productive land and water needed to support a human lifestyle

questions.question: The IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change) is responsible for:
options: Assessing scientific information related to climate change for policymakers
questions.answer: Assessing scientific information related to climate change for policymakers

questions.question: Which water treatment process uses membranes to remove salt from seawater?
questions.answer: Reverse osmosis (desalination)

questions.question: Compare two different renewable energy sources — one intermittent (solar or wind) and one dispatchable (hydro or geothermal). What are the main advantages and challenges of each, and how might they complement each other in a reliable energy grid?
questions.answer: Solar energy is intermittent — it generates electricity only when the sun shines, making it variable and dependent on weather and time of day. Its advantages include zero operational emissions, declining costs, and suitability for distributed generation. Its challenge is that output is unpredictable and storage (batteries) adds cost. Hydroelectric energy is dispatchable — operators can release water on demand to match electricity needs, providing reliable baseload and peak power. Its advantages are long lifespan and low emissions. Its challenge is dependence on rainfall and ecological impacts on rivers and fish. Together, solar can supply cheap daytime electricity while hydroelectric reserves water to dispatch power during cloudy periods or at night, creating a complementary and stable grid.

quizQuestions.question: Environmental science is best described as:
options: An interdisciplinary field studying interactions between humans and the natural world
The study of space and planets
A field concerned only with animals
quizQuestions.answer: An interdisciplinary field studying interactions between humans and the natural world

quizQuestions.question: A hypothesis in the scientific method is:
options: A testable, falsifiable explanation for an observation
A theory supported by all scientists
quizQuestions.answer: A testable, falsifiable explanation for an observation

quizQuestions.question: Systems thinking in environmental science emphasizes:
options: Studying one component at a time
How parts of a system interact and affect each other
quizQuestions.answer: How parts of a system interact and affect each other

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following was a landmark event in U.S. environmental history?
options: The passage of the Clean Air Act in 1970
The invention of the automobile in 1885
quizQuestions.answer: The passage of the Clean Air Act in 1970

quizQuestions.question: A control group in a scientific experiment:
options: Is exposed to all variables simultaneously
Remains unchanged and is used as a baseline for comparison
quizQuestions.answer: Remains unchanged and is used as a baseline for comparison

quizQuestions.question: Which term describes all the organisms living in an area together?
quizQuestions.answer: Community

quizQuestions.question: Energy flows through an ecosystem starting with:
quizQuestions.answer: Producers that capture solar energy

quizQuestions.question: Which biome is characterized by the highest biodiversity?
quizQuestions.answer: Tropical rainforest

quizQuestions.question: An abiotic factor in an aquatic ecosystem is:
quizQuestions.answer: Water temperature and dissolved oxygen

quizQuestions.question: In a food chain, the 10% energy rule means tertiary consumers receive approximately what percentage of the energy originally captured by producers?
Exponential population growth occurs when:
options: The birth rate equals the death rate
Birth rates exceed death rates with unlimited resources
The population has reached carrying capacity
quizQuestions.answer: Birth rates exceed death rates with unlimited resources

quizQuestions.question: Which factor is a density-dependent check on population growth?
options: Disease spreading more easily in a crowded population
quizQuestions.answer: Disease spreading more easily in a crowded population

quizQuestions.question: The demographic transition model predicts that as countries industrialize, birth rates will:
options: Eventually decline to match falling death rates
quizQuestions.answer: Eventually decline to match falling death rates

quizQuestions.question: Overshoot occurs when a population:
options: Exceeds carrying capacity, often leading to a crash
quizQuestions.answer: Exceeds carrying capacity, often leading to a crash

quizQuestions.question: The human population reached 8 billion in approximately:
Which of the following represents the greatest threat to global biodiversity today?
quizQuestions.answer: Habitat destruction and fragmentation

quizQuestions.question: A species is classified as endangered when:
options: It is found only in captivity
It faces a very high risk of extinction in the wild in the near future
It lives in a protected area
quizQuestions.answer: It faces a very high risk of extinction in the wild in the near future

quizQuestions.question: Biodiversity hotspots are regions characterized by:
options: High species richness and high levels of habitat loss
Low temperatures and low human activity
quizQuestions.answer: High species richness and high levels of habitat loss

quizQuestions.question: Ex-situ conservation means:
options: Protecting species in their natural habitat
Conserving species outside their natural environment (e.g., zoos, seed banks)
Removing invasive species from an ecosystem
quizQuestions.answer: Conserving species outside their natural environment (e.g., zoos, seed banks)

quizQuestions.question: Which international agreement regulates trade in endangered species?
options: CITES (Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species)
quizQuestions.answer: CITES (Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species)

quizQuestions.question: Soil is formed through the process of:
options: Weathering of rock combined with accumulation of organic matter over time
quizQuestions.answer: Weathering of rock combined with accumulation of organic matter over time

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT a soil horizon?
quizQuestions.answer: D horizon (deep water)

quizQuestions.question: Desertification is best described as:
options: The natural formation of deserts over millions of years
The process by which productive land degrades into desert-like conditions due to drought and poor land management
quizQuestions.answer: The process by which productive land degrades into desert-like conditions due to drought and poor land management

quizQuestions.question: Crop rotation helps maintain soil health by:
options: Growing the same crop each year to maximize yield
Alternating crops to restore nutrients and break pest cycles
Adding chemical fertilizers after every harvest
quizQuestions.answer: Alternating crops to restore nutrients and break pest cycles

quizQuestions.question: Organic matter in soil is important because it:
options: Improves water retention, nutrient availability, and microbial activity
quizQuestions.answer: Improves water retention, nutrient availability, and microbial activity

quizQuestions.question: The majority of Earth's freshwater is stored in:
quizQuestions.answer: Glaciers and ice caps

quizQuestions.question: Groundwater is replenished through:
options: Infiltration of rainwater through soil into aquifers
quizQuestions.answer: Infiltration of rainwater through soil into aquifers

quizQuestions.question: Eutrophication in lakes is caused primarily by:
options: Nitrogen and phosphorus runoff causing algal blooms that deplete oxygen
quizQuestions.answer: Nitrogen and phosphorus runoff causing algal blooms that deplete oxygen

quizQuestions.question: Which water conservation practice saves the most water in agriculture?
options: Drip irrigation delivering water directly to root zones
quizQuestions.answer: Drip irrigation delivering water directly to root zones

quizQuestions.question: The water table refers to:
options: The surface of a lake or river
The upper boundary of the saturated zone of groundwater
The depth at which bedrock begins
The level of water in a dam reservoir
quizQuestions.answer: The upper boundary of the saturated zone of groundwater

quizQuestions.question: Which layer of the atmosphere contains most of the weather?
quizQuestions.answer: Troposphere

quizQuestions.question: Ground-level ozone (smog) forms when:
options: Nitrogen oxides and VOCs react in sunlight
quizQuestions.answer: Nitrogen oxides and VOCs react in sunlight

quizQuestions.question: Acid rain primarily damages ecosystems by:
options: Increasing soil and water pH above 7
Lowering the pH of lakes and soils, harming aquatic life and trees
quizQuestions.answer: Lowering the pH of lakes and soils, harming aquatic life and trees

quizQuestions.question: The Montreal Protocol (1987) successfully addressed:
options: The release of CFCs that deplete stratospheric ozone
quizQuestions.answer: The release of CFCs that deplete stratospheric ozone

quizQuestions.question: Particulate matter (PM2.5) is dangerous primarily because:
options: It reacts with ozone to form smog
Its tiny size allows it to penetrate deep into lungs and enter the bloodstream
It reflects sunlight and cools the atmosphere
quizQuestions.answer: Its tiny size allows it to penetrate deep into lungs and enter the bloodstream

quizQuestions.question: Which gas is the most abundant greenhouse gas in Earth's atmosphere?
quizQuestions.answer: Water vapor

quizQuestions.question: The difference between weather and climate is:
options: Weather is long-term averages; climate is day-to-day conditions
Climate is what you get; weather is what you expect
Weather refers to short-term atmospheric conditions; climate is the long-term average pattern
quizQuestions.answer: Weather refers to short-term atmospheric conditions; climate is the long-term average pattern

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a direct piece of evidence for climate change?
options: Increased hurricane names on the list
Rising global average temperatures recorded by weather stations and satellites
More news coverage of natural disasters
quizQuestions.answer: Rising global average temperatures recorded by weather stations and satellites

quizQuestions.question: CO₂ concentrations in the atmosphere are measured at the Mauna Loa Observatory and show a:
options: Gradual rise since industrialization with seasonal oscillations
Sharp drop after the Paris Agreement
quizQuestions.answer: Gradual rise since industrialization with seasonal oscillations

quizQuestions.question: Which sector is the largest source of greenhouse gas emissions globally?
options: Energy (burning fossil fuels for electricity and heat)
quizQuestions.answer: Energy (burning fossil fuels for electricity and heat)

quizQuestions.question: Sea level rise threatens coastal communities primarily through:
options: Flooding, saltwater intrusion into freshwater supplies, and more severe storm surges
quizQuestions.answer: Flooding, saltwater intrusion into freshwater supplies, and more severe storm surges

quizQuestions.question: Climate refugees are people who:
options: Voluntarily move to escape cold winters
Are displaced from their homes due to climate change impacts like floods and droughts
Relocate for economic opportunities in green industries
Move to be closer to national parks
quizQuestions.answer: Are displaced from their homes due to climate change impacts like floods and droughts

quizQuestions.question: The Kyoto Protocol differed from the Paris Agreement in that it:
options: Required only developing nations to cut emissions
Set legally binding emissions reduction targets for developed countries
Did not involve the United Nations
quizQuestions.answer: Set legally binding emissions reduction targets for developed countries

quizQuestions.question: Ocean acidification occurs when:
options: CO₂ dissolves in seawater, forming carbonic acid and lowering pH
Plastic pollution releases acid into seawater
quizQuestions.answer: CO₂ dissolves in seawater, forming carbonic acid and lowering pH

quizQuestions.question: Which adaptation strategy helps cities cope with increased flooding from climate change?
options: Installing green infrastructure like permeable pavements and constructed wetlands
quizQuestions.answer: Installing green infrastructure like permeable pavements and constructed wetlands

quizQuestions.question: Coal, oil, and natural gas are called fossil fuels because they:
options: Formed from compressed organic material over millions of years
quizQuestions.answer: Formed from compressed organic material over millions of years

quizQuestions.question: Which fossil fuel emits the most CO₂ per unit of energy produced?
quizQuestions.answer: Coal

quizQuestions.question: Hydraulic fracturing (fracking) is used to:
options: Extract oil and natural gas from tight rock formations using high-pressure fluid
quizQuestions.answer: Extract oil and natural gas from tight rock formations using high-pressure fluid

quizQuestions.question: The main environmental concern with oil extraction offshore is:
options: Risk of oil spills that devastate marine ecosystems
quizQuestions.answer: Risk of oil spills that devastate marine ecosystems

quizQuestions.question: Natural gas is considered 'cleaner' than coal primarily because:
options: It releases roughly half the CO₂ per unit of energy and lower levels of particulate matter
It releases no methane during extraction
quizQuestions.answer: It releases roughly half the CO₂ per unit of energy and lower levels of particulate matter

quizQuestions.question: Photovoltaic (PV) solar panels work by:
options: Heating water to drive a turbine
Converting sunlight directly into electricity using semiconductor materials
Focusing sunlight to melt salt for heat storage
Using sunlight to split hydrogen from water
quizQuestions.answer: Converting sunlight directly into electricity using semiconductor materials

quizQuestions.question: A major limitation of wind and solar energy is:
options: They are intermittent and depend on weather conditions
They are more expensive than coal in all markets
They cannot connect to the electrical grid
quizQuestions.answer: They are intermittent and depend on weather conditions

quizQuestions.question: Hydroelectric power generates electricity by:
options: Using flowing or falling water to spin turbines connected to generators
Splitting water molecules with solar energy
quizQuestions.answer: Using flowing or falling water to spin turbines connected to generators

quizQuestions.question: Nuclear energy produces electricity through:
options: Fission — splitting heavy atoms to release heat that drives a steam turbine
Fusion of hydrogen atoms at room temperature
Chemical reactions between radioactive materials and water
quizQuestions.answer: Fission — splitting heavy atoms to release heat that drives a steam turbine

quizQuestions.question: Which renewable energy source is most suitable for Iceland due to its location on a volcanic hotspot?
quizQuestions.answer: Geothermal

quizQuestions.question: A point source of pollution is:
options: Runoff from agricultural fields across a watershed
A specific, identifiable location where pollution is discharged (e.g., a factory pipe)
Atmospheric pollution with no traceable origin
Pollution from many small sources combined
quizQuestions.answer: A specific, identifiable location where pollution is discharged (e.g., a factory pipe)

quizQuestions.question: Ocean plastic pollution is dangerous because:
options: It breaks into microplastics ingested by marine organisms and accumulates up the food chain
It reflects sunlight, causing ocean cooling
quizQuestions.answer: It breaks into microplastics ingested by marine organisms and accumulates up the food chain

quizQuestions.question: Biological oxygen demand (BOD) is used to measure:
options: The amount of dissolved oxygen fish need
The level of organic pollution in water, indicating how much oxygen microbes need to decompose it
The concentration of heavy metals in water
quizQuestions.answer: The level of organic pollution in water, indicating how much oxygen microbes need to decompose it

quizQuestions.question: Which type of pollution comes from diffuse sources that are difficult to trace to a single origin?
quizQuestions.answer: Non-point source pollution

quizQuestions.question: Heavy metal contamination of soil and water is particularly dangerous because heavy metals:
options: Quickly break down in the environment
Persist in the environment, bioaccumulate in tissues, and are toxic at low concentrations
quizQuestions.answer: Persist in the environment, bioaccumulate in tissues, and are toxic at low concentrations

quizQuestions.question: The hierarchy of waste management from most to least preferred is:
quizQuestions.answer: Reduce, reuse, recycle, dispose

quizQuestions.question: Modern sanitary landfills are designed to:
options: Allow waste to decompose freely in open air
Contain waste using liners and leachate collection systems to prevent groundwater contamination
Sort recyclables automatically from mixed waste
quizQuestions.answer: Contain waste using liners and leachate collection systems to prevent groundwater contamination

quizQuestions.question: A life-cycle analysis (LCA) evaluates:
options: Only the cost of a product at the point of sale
The total environmental impact of a product from raw material extraction through disposal
The lifespan of a product before it breaks
quizQuestions.answer: The total environmental impact of a product from raw material extraction through disposal

quizQuestions.question: E-waste (electronic waste) is hazardous because it contains:
options: Toxic materials including lead, mercury, and cadmium
quizQuestions.answer: Toxic materials including lead, mercury, and cadmium

quizQuestions.question: Composting is a form of waste management that:
options: Burns organic waste to generate electricity
Converts organic waste into nutrient-rich soil amendment through decomposition
Compresses waste into smaller volumes for landfills
quizQuestions.answer: Converts organic waste into nutrient-rich soil amendment through decomposition

quizQuestions.question: The United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) include all EXCEPT:
quizQuestions.answer: Unlimited industrial growth

quizQuestions.question: Green technology refers to:
options: Technology painted green for environmental awareness
Products and processes designed to minimize environmental impact and conserve resources
Technology used only in national parks
quizQuestions.answer: Products and processes designed to minimize environmental impact and conserve resources

quizQuestions.question: Environmental justice focuses on ensuring that:
options: Only wealthy communities benefit from environmental regulations
Low-income and minority communities bear a disproportionate share of environmental burdens
All communities share environmental benefits and burdens equally
Only global environmental issues are addressed
quizQuestions.answer: All communities share environmental benefits and burdens equally

quizQuestions.question: Which individual action has the largest positive environmental impact?
options: Reducing consumption of animal products and driving less (or switching to EVs)
quizQuestions.answer: Reducing consumption of animal products and driving less (or switching to EVs)

quizQuestions.question: Regenerative agriculture differs from conventional farming in that it:
options: Uses more pesticides for higher yield
Focuses solely on maximizing output per hectare
Aims to restore soil health, sequester carbon, and enhance biodiversity while producing food
quizQuestions.answer: Aims to restore soil health, sequester carbon, and enhance biodiversity while producing food
